question: Cosmic ray spectrum: what's the broad shape and what are 'knee' and 'ankle' features?

options: Power-law $dN/dE \propto E^{-\gamma}$ over 11 decades in energy ($10^9-10^{20}$ eV). Knee at $\sim 3\times 10^{15}$ eV ($\gamma$ steepens from 2.7 to 3.1): galactic confinement breakdown for protons. Ankle at $\sim 5\times 10^{18}$ eV ($\gamma$ flattens): transition to extragalactic component. GZK cutoff $\sim 5\times 10^{19}$ eV
Flat spectrum at all energies (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)

Cuts off sharply at 10 TeV (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses) (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder)
No spectrum; cosmic rays monoenergetic (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements)
passive.explanation: Discovered by Hess (1912 balloon flight, Nobel 1936). Composition: $\sim 90\%$ protons, $\sim 9\%$ alpha, $\sim 1\%$ heavier nuclei + electrons + photons. Detector techniques: direct (balloons, satellites — AMS-02, PAMELA) up to $\sim$ TeV; indirect (extensive air showers — Auger, Telescope Array, HAWC, LHAASO) above. Spectral features: knee from galactic SNR acceleration limit (max rigidity $E_{\max}/Z \sim 10^{15}$ eV from Hillas criterion); ankle from extragalactic dominance + pair-production losses on CMB; cutoff from GZK (proton-CMB photo-pion production). Composition transitions: knee region $p \to$ heavier; ankle transition still debated.

active.prompt: Match each CR energy region with its astrophysics:

pairs.definition: Galactic supernova remnants; diffusive shock acceleration; first-order Fermi mechanism
Galactic confinement breaks down; max energy from Hillas $E_{\max} \sim ZeBL/c$ for SNRs
Extragalactic dominance starts; transition probably from galactic Fe to extragalactic light
Extragalactic; sources unknown (AGN? GRBs? starburst?); GZK suppression; Auger composition mixed

active.explanation: Cosmic-ray origin still an open question above the ankle. Anisotropy: Auger detected dipole at $E > 8$ EeV pointing $\sim 125°$ from Galactic centre, consistent with extragalactic. Correlations with nearby starburst galaxies hinted (2$\sigma$). Multi-messenger combined with $\nu$, $\gamma$ promising.

question: Fermi acceleration: how do shocks produce cosmic-ray power-law spectrum?

options: 1st-order Fermi (diffusive shock acceleration): charged particles scatter back-and-forth across shock front, gaining $\Delta E/E \sim v_{\rm shock}/c$ per crossing; combined with finite escape probability gives universal spectral index $\gamma = 2$ at strong-shock limit ($u_1/u_2 = 4$ for monatomic gas). Naturally explains observed $\gamma \sim 2.7$ after propagation losses

Acceleration by pure thermal heating only (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables) (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)

Spectrum exponential, not power-law (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses) (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling)
Fermi acceleration produces monoenergetic CRs (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD)
passive.explanation: Fermi (1949) original mechanism: 2nd order (scattering off magnetic clouds), $\Delta E/E \sim (v/c)^2$ — too slow. Modern: 1st-order at shock fronts (Krymskii, Bell, Axford 1970s). Charged particle in turbulent magnetic field crosses shock; in downstream frame, picks up momentum $\sim u/c$ per cycle. Statistical: particle accelerated through many cycles, finite escape probability $P_{\rm esc}$, gives differential energy spectrum $dN/dE \propto E^{-\gamma}$ with $\gamma = 1 + \ln(1/P_{\rm ret})/\ln(E_{\rm out}/E_{\rm in}) \to 2$ in strong-shock limit. Observed at source: $\gamma_{\rm source} \approx 2.0-2.2$. Propagation losses (escape from Milky Way disk, energy losses): steepen to observed $\gamma \sim 2.7$. Confirmed by gamma-ray observations of SNRs (Fermi-LAT, MAGIC, HESS) showing pion-decay signature.

question: Air showers: how do ultra-high-energy cosmic rays interact with the atmosphere?

options: Primary CR initiates hadronic cascade: pion production in inelastic collisions, $\pi^0\to\gamma\gamma$ feeds electromagnetic sub-cascade (pair production + bremsstrahlung); $\pi^\pm$ decay to muons. Shower peaks at $X_{\max} \sim 750-800$ g/cm$^2$ depth; particle count $\sim$ $E_0$/(critical energy of air). Detected via fluorescence (Auger, TA), Cherenkov, particle arrays at ground

Primary CR makes single hole through atmosphere (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements) (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder) (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling)

No interaction with atmosphere; passes straight through (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD) (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)

Showers contain only photons, no hadrons (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses) (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data)
passive.explanation: Air shower physics: hadronic primary (proton, nucleus) hits N$_2$/O$_2$ nucleus around $\sim 50-100$ g/cm$^2$ depth (top of atmosphere); produces $\sim 50$ pions per interaction. $\pi^0$ ($\tau = 8\times 10^{-17}$ s) decays immediately to $\gamma\gamma$ — feeds EM cascade dominated by pair production + bremsstrahlung. $\pi^\pm$ ($\tau = 26$ ns) compete decay vs interaction depending on energy: high-$E$ interact further, low-$E$ decay to $\mu\nu$. Muons reach ground (high penetrating, $\sim 70\,\mu$s lab lifetime). Shower max depth $X_{\max}$: depends logarithmically on energy, sensitive to primary composition (heavier $\Rightarrow$ shallower $X_{\max}$). Detection: (i) Surface arrays (water Cherenkov tanks for Auger, scintillators for TA); (ii) Fluorescence telescopes (UV from $N_2$ excitation by shower electrons) — measures longitudinal profile directly; (iii) Radio (LOFAR, GRAND): coherent geomagnetic emission.

question: IceCube detected astrophysical neutrinos. What are the sources and observed properties?

options: Cubic-km detector at South Pole; observes $\sim 100$ astrophysical $\nu$/yr above 100 TeV; nearly isotropic distribution; spectrum $\propto E^{-2.5}$ — softer than expected; sources include blazar TXS 0506+056 (2017 coincident with $\gamma$-ray flare), galactic plane diffuse emission (2023), starburst galaxy NGC 1068 (2022). Multi-messenger era opened

IceCube detects no astrophysical neutrinos (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables) (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)

Detected only solar neutrinos (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses) (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (contradicted by both lattice simulations and dispersive analyses)
Sources all identified as cosmic ray sources directly (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements)
passive.explanation:
IceCube: km$^3$ of Antarctic ice instrumented with 5160 photomultiplier modules (DOMs); detects $
u$ via Cherenkov from secondary $\mu, e$ tracks. Discovery: 2013, first observation of astrophysical $
u$ above 100 TeV (Aartsen et al.). Flux: $E^2\Phi \sim 10^{-8}$ GeV/cm²/s/sr at $\sim 100$ TeV. Highest event: $\sim 10$ PeV. Most events 'cascade' (NC + $
u_e$ CC: spherical light); some 'tracks' ($
u_\mu$ CC: extended muon) with $\sim 1°$ pointing. Source identifications: (1) TXS 0506+056 (Sep 2017): IceCube alert + Fermi/MAGIC gamma flare — 3$\sigma$ blazar association; (2) NGC 1068 (2022): 4.2$\sigma$ steady $
u$ source; (3) Galactic plane (June 2023): 4.5$\sigma$ excess from galactic diffuse emission. Implications: cosmic accelerators producing both pions ($	o
u$) and gamma rays; gradually identifying CR origins.

question: Multi-messenger astronomy: how do GW + neutrinos + photons combine?

options: Different messengers probe different phenomena: photons (any EM, but obscured by dust); neutrinos (penetrate dense regions, point to hadronic processes); GW (probe gravitational dynamics); cosmic rays (charged, scrambled by magnetic fields, no direct pointing). Combined: NS-NS merger GW170817 + GRB + kilonova; blazar TXS 0506 IceCube $\nu$ + Fermi $\gamma$; future GW + $\nu$ from core-collapse SN

All messengers identical; no benefit to combination (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data) (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD)

Multi-messenger limited to gamma + radio (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses)

Cosmic rays cannot be combined with anything (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements) (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum) (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder)
passive.explanation:
Multi-messenger era launched by GW170817 (BNS merger, 17 Aug 2017) + GRB 170817A + AT2017gfo kilonova. Each messenger gives unique info: GW = source dynamics, distance via standard-siren; gamma = jet physics; optical/UV/IR = kilonova r-process nucleosynthesis; radio = afterglow + jet structure. Other key events: TXS 0506+056 (Sep 2017, IceCube $
u$ + Fermi $\gamma$ from blazar flare); NS-BH mergers (GW200105, GW200115; some claimed EM but unconfirmed). Future: LIGO O4-O5 + Roman + Rubin + CTA + IceCube-Gen2; supernova in Milky Way (overdue) would give GW + $
u$ (SN 1987A had $
u$ via Kamiokande + IMB, no GW detector existed); coordinated alerts via Astrophysical Multimessenger Observatory Network (AMON), General Coordinates Network (GCN).

question: Ultra-high-energy cosmic ray puzzle: what's the proposed origin and current best evidence?

options: Origin of CRs above $10^{19.5}$ eV unknown but constrained: (i) Hillas criterion + GZK horizon $\sim 100$ Mpc → only nearby AGN (e.g.\ Centaurus A), starburst galaxies, GRBs satisfy; (ii) Auger dipole anisotropy + correlation with nearby starbursts at $\sim 4\sigma$; (iii) composition shifts heavier at $E > 10^{19.3}$ eV — disfavours pure proton from AGN; (iv) recent extreme event $\sim 2.4\times 10^{20}$ eV ('Amaterasu', TA 2023) — unidentified source

UHECR origin solved as supernovae (inconsistent with measured precision tests at LEP, LHC, and lattice QCD) (violates basic gauge invariance and Lorentz covariance requirements) (not consistent with the operator product expansion at large momentum)

All UHECR from active galactic nuclei confirmed (unsupported by perturbative expansions in the coupling) (historically considered but disfavoured by modern QFT analyses) (ruled out by experimental constraints from collider and low-energy data)

Galactic centre is dominant source (would conflict with renormalisation group flow and cross-section data) (would imply unphysical infinite or negative observables) (disagrees with the standard textbook treatment in Peskin-Schroeder)
passive.explanation: Hillas criterion: max energy $E_{\max} \sim Ze \beta B L \sim 10^{20}$ eV requires $B L \gtrsim 10^{17}$ G$\cdot$cm. Candidates: AGN jets/lobes (radio galaxies like Cen A, M87), GRBs, magnetars, tidal disruption events, starburst-galaxy supernovae. GZK horizon: protons above $5\times 10^{19}$ eV lose energy in $< 100$ Mpc → sources must be nearby. Pierre Auger Observatory ($> 17000$ km² in Argentina): 3000 km² array, full sky south; dipole detected $E > 8$ EeV pointing $125°$ from Galactic centre (4$\sigma$). Correlation with nearby starburst catalog: $4.0\sigma$ excess. Composition: from $X_{\max}$ distributions, heavier nuclei above $\sim 10^{19.3}$ eV; complicates origin since heavies have smaller propagation horizon. Telescope Array (US): northern hemisphere, complementary; 2023 reported '244 EeV' event — too high for any known source within GZK horizon. Future: AugerPrime upgrade (improved composition sensing), POEMMA satellite, GRAND.
